title: Sequence model
summary: A model family that reads ordered inputs—tokens, audio frames, or time steps—and updates a hidden state along the sequence axis.
openingSummary: Sequence models treat order as first-class: each step sees earlier context through recurrence, convolution, state-space dynamics, or attention rather than assuming inputs are an unordered bag.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A sequence model consumes data with a meaningful order—text tokens left to right, audio frames in time, or sensor readings along a trajectory. It produces per-step outputs or a final summary while respecting that order through masked attention, recurrent state, convolution over time, or structured state updates. The family name describes the input geometry, not any one parameter count.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Most language, speech, and time-series systems are sequence models under the hood. Separating the family from specific layouts—decoder-only transformers versus RNNs—helps readers choose module pages for context mixing and spot when a product label hides a simpler recurrent core.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A speech recognizer ingests 10 ms frames sequentially; each frame updates hidden vectors that later emit phoneme logits. A chat model is also a sequence model: it embeds prompt tokens in order and predicts the next token from prior context, usually with causal attention in a decoder stack.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Sequence model names the input setting, not a training objective—you still need a loss and data. It is broader than transformer: transformers are one popular sequence family, not the definition of sequencing. It also differs from autoregressive generation, which describes a decoding loop that many—but not all—sequence models use.
